display_title: Soi Nhụ culture appeared
summary: The Soi Nhụ culture appeared.

display_title: Hoabinhian artifacts began to be produced in Northern Vietnam
summary: Hoabinhian artifacts began to be produced in Northern Vietnam.

display_title: Kinh Dương Vương unified all vassal states in his territory into the single state of Xích Quỷ, which he rul...
summary: Kinh Dương Vương unified all vassal states in his territory into the single state of Xích Quỷ, which he ruled as Hùng king from the capital at Phong Châu. He also sponsored the development of martial arts in Xích Quỷ at the time.

display_title: Đông Sơn culture appeared in the valley of the Red River
historical_status: Legendary/traditional dates; cultural developments vary by source
summary: The Đông Sơn culture appeared in the valley of the Red River.

display_title: Văn Lang likely formed as a Lạc Việt kingdom
historical_status: Legendary/traditional dates; cultural developments vary by source
summary: Modern research and archaeological evidence are described as broadly supporting a Văn Lang kingdom around the 7th century BCE.

display_title: Văn Lang organized a 15-bộ administrative system
historical_status: Legendary/traditional dates; cultural developments vary by source
summary: Văn Lang's early monarchy was described as having Hùng kings, Lạc hầu, Lạc tướng and 15 bộ, with villages managed by Bồ chính.

display_title: Thục Phán, ruler of the Âu Việt, invaded and conquered Văn Lang
summary: Thục Phán, ruler of the Âu Việt, invaded and conquered Văn Lang. He renamed the country Âu Lạc and took the regnal name An Dương Vương, ruling as king from Cổ Loa Citadel.

display_title: Âu Việt–Lạc Việt alliance resisted Qin invasion
summary: The source states that the Âu Việt–Lạc Việt alliance defeated a Qin invasion before or during the formation of Âu Lạc.

display_title: Triệu Đà conquered Âu Lạc
historical_status: Historical dates from timeline; verify details
summary: Triệu Đà annexed Âu Lạc; the source gives 208 BCE or 179 BCE as possible dates.

display_title: Zhao Tuo declared himself king of Nanyue, with his capital in modern Panyu, Guangzhou
historical_status: Historical dates from timeline; verify details
summary: Zhao Tuo declared himself king of Nanyue, with his capital in modern Panyu, Guangzhou.

display_title: Han conquest of Nanyue
summary: Han conquest of Nanyue: Han forces invaded Nanyue. Zhao Jiande was captured in flight and executed. The zhou of Jiaozhou was organized on the territory of the defunct Nanyue and divided into the commanderies of Nanhai, Cangwu, Yulin, Jiaozhi, Hepu, Zhuya, Taner, and Jiuzhen. Shi Dai was appointed its governor.

display_title: Han assimilation policies intensified in Giao Chỉ
summary: The Han administration increased taxation and altered social/legal structures as part of assimilation policies.

display_title: Trung sisters' rebellion
summary: Trung sisters' rebellion: The Trưng Sisters launched a rebellion against Han authority in the Red River Delta.

display_title: Trung sisters' rebellion
people: Trưng Trắc; Trưng Nhị; Ma Yuan
summary: Trung sisters' rebellion: the Trưng Sisters committed suicide by drowning themselves before The Han general Ma Yuan could capture them.

display_title: Mã Viện suppressed the Trưng Sisters uprising
summary: Han general Mã Viện suppressed the Trưng Sisters' uprising after three years of autonomy.

display_title: Sun Quan declared himself emperor, founding the state of Eastern Wu, separating from the Han dynasty
summary: Sun Quan declared himself emperor, founding the state of Eastern Wu, separating from the Han dynasty.

display_title: Lady Triệu uprising
summary: Lady Triệu uprising.

display_title: Lý Bí uprising began against Liang rule
summary: Lý Bí rose against Liang rule in 541, preceding the establishment of Vạn Xuân in 544.

period_name: Vạn Xuân / Early Lý and Triệu Việt Vương
summary: Following his rebellion and expulsion of Liang forces from Jiaozhou, Lý Nam Đế was proclaimed emperor of Vạn Xuân.

period_name: Vạn Xuân / Early Lý and Triệu Việt Vương

locations: Gia Ninh Citadel; Long Biên; Việt Trì
summary: The Liang general Emperor Wu of Chen launched a surprise attack on the Vạn Xuân capital Long Biên, forcing Lý Nam Đế and the imperial administration to flee to the Gia Ninh Citadel in modern Việt Trì.

display_title: Lý Nam Đế was forced to retreat to Khuất Lão Cave, where he reorganized his army under the command of Triệu...
period_name: Vạn Xuân / Early Lý and Triệu Việt Vương
people: Triệu Việt Vương; Lý Nam Đế
summary: Lý Nam Đế was forced to retreat to Khuất Lão Cave, where he reorganized his army under the command of Triệu Việt Vương.

display_title: Vạn Xuân forces defended Dạ Trạch in modern Khoái Châu District from Liang forces
period_name: Vạn Xuân / Early Lý and Triệu Việt Vương
summary: Vạn Xuân forces defended Dạ Trạch in modern Khoái Châu District from Liang forces.

display_title: Lý Nam Đế was assassinated in modern Laos
period_name: Vạn Xuân / Early Lý and Triệu Việt Vương
summary: Lý Nam Đế was assassinated in modern Laos.

display_title: Triệu Việt Vương expelled Liang forces from Vạn Xuân and reestablished the capital at Long Biên
period_name: Vạn Xuân / Early Lý and Triệu Việt Vương
summary: Triệu Việt Vương expelled Liang forces from Vạn Xuân and reestablished the capital at Long Biên.

display_title: Khúc Thừa Dụ occupied Đại La and began Vietnamese autonomy
summary: Khúc Thừa Dụ took control of Đại La as Tang power weakened, beginning a period of Vietnamese self-rule.

display_title: Battle of Bạch Đằng
period_name: Ngô dynasty and Twelve Warlords transition
summary: Battle of Bạch Đằng: Ngô Quyền defeated the Southern Han kingdom at the Battle of Bạch Đằng north of modern Haiphong and ended 1,000 years of Chinese domination dating back to 111 BC under the Han dynasty, founding the Ngô dynasty.

display_title: Ngô Quyền proclaimed himself king
period_name: Ngô dynasty and Twelve Warlords transition
summary: After defeating Southern Han, Ngô Quyền proclaimed himself king in 939.

display_title: Đinh Bộ Lĩnh founded Đại Cồ Việt
summary: Đinh Bộ Lĩnh proclaimed himself emperor, founded the Đinh dynasty and named the country Đại Cồ Việt.

display_title: Emperor Đinh Bộ Lĩnh of Đại Cồ Việt was assassinated along with his crown prince Đinh Liễn by a minor palac...
people: Đinh Bộ Lĩnh; Đinh Phế Đế; Đinh Liễn; Lê Hoàn
summary: Emperor Đinh Bộ Lĩnh of Đại Cồ Việt was assassinated along with his crown prince Đinh Liễn by a minor palace official. His surviving son, the young Đinh Phế Đế, succeeded him under the regency of the commander-in-chief Lê Hoàn.

display_title: Lê Hoàn founded the Early Lê dynasty
summary: Lê Hoàn came to the throne and founded the Early Lê dynasty.

display_title: Lê Hoàn declared himself emperor at Hoa Lư
summary: Lê Hoàn declared himself emperor at Hoa Lư.

display_title: Nam tiến expanded Đại Việt southward to Cù Mông
period_name: Later Lê dynasty / early consolidated Lê
summary: Through war and political marriage with Champa, Đại Việt expanded from Hoành Sơn toward Cù Mông from the 11th to 15th centuries.

display_title: Lý Thánh Tông renamed the country Đại Việt
summary: Lý Thánh Tông changed the country name to Đại Việt in 1054.

display_title: Mongol invasions of Vietnam
locations: Cổ Loa Citadel; Tống Bình / Đại La; Thăng Long; Hanoi; Temple of Literature, Hanoi; Đông Quan; Đông Kinh; Ngọc Hồi-Đống Đa
summary: Mongol invasions of Vietnam: The Mongol Empire invaded Đại Việt and conquered the capital at modern Hanoi. Trần Thái Tông fled to an island.

display_title: Mongol invasions of Vietnam
summary: Mongol invasions of Vietnam: The Đại Việt commander-in-chief Trần Hưng Đạo drew out and harassed a Yuan invasion force, forcing their retreat.

display_title: Mongol invasions of Vietnam
summary: Mongol invasions of Vietnam: The Mongol navy was destroyed, forcing the army, left without provisions, to begin its retreat from Đại Việt.

display_title: Hồ Quý Ly overthrew the Đại Việt emperor, enthroned himself, renamed the country Đại Ngu and moved the capi...
summary: Hồ Quý Ly overthrew the Đại Việt emperor, enthroned himself, renamed the country Đại Ngu and moved the capital to the citadel of the Hồ dynasty.

display_title: Ming–Hồ War
summary: Ming–Hồ War: Ming forces captured the Đại Ngu capitals.

display_title: Ming occupation of Đại Việt began
period_name: Ming occupation and Lam Sơn uprising
summary: Ming forces invaded Đại Ngu and began the fourth period of Chinese domination.

display_title: Lê Lợi was declared emperor of an independent Đại Việt
period_name: Later Lê dynasty / early consolidated Lê
summary: Lê Lợi was declared emperor of an independent Đại Việt.

display_title: Mạc Đăng Dung forced Emperor Lê Cung Hoàng to abdicate, establishing the Mạc dynasty
people: Lê Cung Hoàng; Mạc Đăng Dung
summary: Mạc Đăng Dung forced Emperor Lê Cung Hoàng to abdicate, establishing the Mạc dynasty.

display_title: Lê Trung Hưng restored with Nguyễn Kim's support
summary: The Later Lê restoration began in 1533 with help from Nguyễn Kim, controlling territory from Thanh Hóa toward Bình Định.

display_title: Nguyễn Hoàng entered Thuận Hóa and laid foundation for Đàng Trong
summary: Nguyễn Hoàng moved to Thuận Hóa, beginning the process that later formed the Nguyễn lords' southern base.

display_title: Global trade expanded in Đàng Ngoài and Đàng Trong
summary: European, Japanese and Chinese merchants traded in Đại Việt, including at Kẻ Chợ and Faifo/Hội An.

display_title: Nguyễn administration began exploiting and controlling Hoàng Sa
summary: The source states that Hoàng Sa was exploited and controlled from the early 17th century.

display_title: Đàng Ngoài and Đàng Trong divided along the Gianh River
summary: The Trịnh–Nguyễn conflict produced two separate political zones, Đàng Ngoài and Đàng Trong, divided by the Gianh River.

display_title: Nguyễn lords annexed remaining Champa territory
summary: The Nguyễn lords annexed the remaining Cham territory from Phú Yên to Bình Thuận in 1693.

display_title: Nguyễn lords established control over Nam Bộ
summary: From 1698 to 1757, the Nguyễn administration gradually established control over Nam Bộ.

display_title: Tây Sơn uprising began in Quy Nhơn
summary: The Tây Sơn uprising began in Quy Nhơn and grew into a movement that defeated the Nguyễn and Trịnh regimes.

display_title: Nguyễn Văn Nhạc proclaimed himself emperor of Đại Việt with his capital at Quy Nhơn
summary: Nguyễn Văn Nhạc proclaimed himself emperor of Đại Việt with his capital at Quy Nhơn.

display_title: Tây Sơn movement ended the Trịnh–Nguyễn division
summary: The Tây Sơn movement defeated both northern and southern ruling houses, ending the long division of the country.

display_title: Nguyễn Văn Nhạc's younger brother Nguyễn Văn Huệ proclaimed himself emperor of Đại Việt
people: Nguyễn Văn Nhạc; Nguyễn Văn Huệ
summary: Nguyễn Văn Nhạc's younger brother Nguyễn Văn Huệ proclaimed himself emperor of Đại Việt. Nguyễn Văn Nhạc relinquished the title, taking that of king instead.

display_title: Emperor Cảnh Thịnh died, Emperor Gia Long became ruler of Vietnam
people: Nguyễn Thế Tổ; Nguyễn Quang Toản
summary: Emperor Cảnh Thịnh died, Emperor Gia Long became ruler of Vietnam.

display_title: Nguyễn dynasty built and ruled from Huế
summary: Gia Long established Huế as the Nguyễn capital and built a centralized monarchy continued by Minh Mạng.

display_title: Gia Long changed the country name to Việt Nam
summary: Gia Long changed the country name from Đại Việt to Việt Nam in 1804.

display_title: Emperor Gia Long (1802–1820) ended his rule of Vietnam
summary: Emperor Gia Long (1802–1820) ended his rule of Vietnam.

display_title: Nguyễn dynasty asserted control over Trường Sa
summary: The source states that Trường Sa was controlled from 1816.

display_title: Nguyễn court restricted Catholicism and foreign trade
summary: The Nguyễn court under Minh Mạng, Thiệu Trị and Tự Đức restricted Catholicism and continued limits on foreign trade.

display_title: Emperor Minh Mạng (1820–1841) ended his rule of Vietnam
summary: Emperor Minh Mạng (1820–1841) ended his rule of Vietnam.

display_title: Emperor Thiệu Trị (1841–1847) ended his rule of Vietnam
summary: Emperor Thiệu Trị (1841–1847) ended his rule of Vietnam.

display_title: French conquest expanded from Nam Kỳ into Bắc Kỳ and Trung Kỳ
period_name: French conquest and early colonial takeover
summary: After taking Nam Kỳ, France expanded into the rest of Vietnam through complex conflicts in Bắc Kỳ from 1873 to 1886.

display_title: French colonial administration was formalized
summary: By 1887, France had organized a relatively complete colonial administration under the Indochina Governor-General system.

display_title: Communist Party of Vietnam (CPV) is the founding and ruling communist party of the Socialist Republic of Vi...
summary: Communist Party of Vietnam (CPV) is the founding and ruling communist party of the Socialist Republic of Vietnam.

display_title: Việt Minh was founded
summary: Việt Minh was founded in 1941 as an independence front led from Pắc Bó by Hồ Chí Minh after his return to Vietnam.

display_title: Japanese coup ended French colonial administration in Indochina
summary: Japan overthrew French colonial administration in March 1945 and backed the Empire of Vietnam.

display_title: Ất Dậu famine devastated northern and central Vietnam
summary: The 1945 famine affected Bắc Kỳ and Trung Kỳ; the source gives an estimate of about two million deaths.

display_title: Empire of Vietnam is established under Japanese sponsorship
period_name: August Revolution and First Indochina War
summary: The Empire of Vietnam is established under Japanese sponsorship.

display_title: August Revolution by the Viet Minh
period_name: August Revolution and First Indochina War
summary: August Revolution by the Viet Minh. Emperor Bảo Đại abdicates. Civil conflicts in Vietnam (1945–1949) begins.

display_title: Allied forces disarm the Japanese in Indochina after World War II, with British-Indian troops operating sou...
period_name: August Revolution and First Indochina War
summary: Allied forces disarm the Japanese in Indochina after World War II, with British-Indian troops operating south of the 16th parallel and Chinese nationalist forces operating north of the 16th parallel.

display_title: Hồ Chí Minh declared the Democratic Republic of Vietnam
period_name: August Revolution and First Indochina War
summary: On 2 September 1945, Hồ Chí Minh declared the independent Democratic Republic of Vietnam in Hanoi.

display_title: First national election of the Democratic Republic of Vietnam
period_name: August Revolution and First Indochina War
summary: A nationwide election was held in early 1946, helping formalize the Democratic Republic of Vietnam's government.

display_title: First Indochina War began
period_name: August Revolution and First Indochina War
summary: War between the Democratic Republic of Vietnam and France broke out in December 1946 after failed negotiations.

display_title: State of Vietnam is established within the French Union
period_name: August Revolution and First Indochina War
summary: The State of Vietnam is established within the French Union.

display_title: China and the Soviet Union began supporting the Democratic Republic of Vietnam
period_name: August Revolution and First Indochina War
summary: In 1950, China and the Soviet Union began supplying weapons to the Democratic Republic of Vietnam, while France was backed by the United States.

display_title: Land reform carried out in North Vietnam
period_name: Partition, North/South Vietnam, and Vietnam War
summary: North Vietnam carried out land reform in the 1950s, redistributing land but also causing social disruption.

display_title: Battle of Dien Bien Phu
period_name: Partition, North/South Vietnam, and Vietnam War
summary: Battle of Dien Bien Phu: French troops begin the battle against the Viet Minh in Dien Bien Phu.

display_title: Battle of Dien Bien Phu ends in a French defeat
period_name: Partition, North/South Vietnam, and Vietnam War
summary: Battle of Dien Bien Phu ends in a French defeat.

display_title: Geneva Conference sends French Union forces to the south, and Viet Minh forces to the north, of a ceasefire...
period_name: Partition, North/South Vietnam, and Vietnam War
summary: The Geneva Conference sends French Union forces to the south, and Viet Minh forces to the north, of a ceasefire line, and calls for elections to decide the government for all of Vietnam by July 1956. Failure to abide by the terms of the agreement leads to the establishment de facto of regimes of North Vietnam and South Vietnam, and the Vietnam War.

display_title: Geneva settlement temporarily divided Vietnam at the 17th parallel
period_name: Partition, North/South Vietnam, and Vietnam War
summary: The Geneva settlement created temporary military regrouping zones north and south of the 17th parallel and called for future elections.

display_title: Post-Geneva migration and regroupment took place
period_name: Partition, North/South Vietnam, and Vietnam War
summary: After Geneva, large population movements and military regroupments took place between North and South Vietnam.

display_title: Ngô Đình Diệm proclaims Vietnam to be a republic with himself as its President (following the State of Viet...
period_name: Partition, North/South Vietnam, and Vietnam War
summary: Ngô Đình Diệm proclaims Vietnam to be a republic with himself as its President (following the State of Vietnam referendum on 23 October) and forms the Army of the Republic of Vietnam.

display_title: Vietnam War begins between North Vietnam and South Vietnam; the north is allied with the Viet Cong
period_name: Partition, North/South Vietnam, and Vietnam War
summary: The Vietnam War begins between North Vietnam and South Vietnam; the north is allied with the Viet Cong.

display_title: Planned 1956 national election did not take place
period_name: Partition, North/South Vietnam, and Vietnam War
summary: The planned post-Geneva election to unify Vietnam did not happen.

display_title: National Liberation Front formed in South Vietnam
period_name: Partition, North/South Vietnam, and Vietnam War
summary: The National Liberation Front was established in South Vietnam with support from North Vietnam.

display_title: American involvement in the Vietnam War officially begins, as the first American helicopters arrive in Saig...
period_name: Partition, North/South Vietnam, and Vietnam War
locations: Gia Định; Saigon; Xá Lợi Pagoda
summary: The American involvement in the Vietnam War officially begins, as the first American helicopters arrive in Saigon along with 400 U.S. personnel.

display_title: The Vietnam War ends with the Fall of Saigon
period_name: Partition, North/South Vietnam, and Vietnam War
locations: North Vietnam; South Vietnam; Gia Định; Saigon; Xá Lợi Pagoda
summary: The Vietnam War ends with the Fall of Saigon: The Vietnam War concludes as Communist forces from North Vietnam take Saigon, resulting in mass evacuation of the remaining American troops and South Vietnam civilians. As the capital is taken, South Vietnam surrenders unconditionally and is replaced with the temporary Provisional Government.

display_title: Khmer Rouge attacks on Phú Quốc and Thổ Chu
period_name: Reunified Socialist Republic and postwar conflicts
summary: Khmer Rouge forces attacked Phú Quốc and Thổ Chu in May 1975.

display_title: Vietnamese border conflicts with Khmer Rouge escalated
period_name: Reunified Socialist Republic and postwar conflicts
summary: Border conflicts with the Khmer Rouge escalated from 1975 to 1978 before Vietnam's counteroffensive.

display_title: National Assembly proclaims unification of the country as the Socialist Republic of Vietnam
period_name: Reunified Socialist Republic and postwar conflicts
summary: The National Assembly proclaims unification of the country as the Socialist Republic of Vietnam.

display_title: 1976 national election unified state institutions
period_name: Reunified Socialist Republic and postwar conflicts
summary: On 25 April 1976, North and South Vietnam held an election to unify state institutions before the proclamation of the Socialist Republic of Vietnam.

display_title: Admittance to United Nations
period_name: Reunified Socialist Republic and postwar conflicts
summary: Admittance to United Nations.

display_title: Postwar boat people migration increased
period_name: Reunified Socialist Republic and postwar conflicts
summary: From 1978, economic hardship, conflict and the Hoa kiều crisis contributed to a wave of people leaving Vietnam by sea or overland.

display_title: Vietnamese forces entered Phnom Penh
period_name: Reunified Socialist Republic and postwar conflicts
summary: Vietnamese forces entered Phnom Penh on 7 January 1979 and drove out the Khmer Rouge government.

display_title: Admittance to ASEAN
summary: Admittance to ASEAN.

display_title: Vietnam normalized relations with the United States
summary: Vietnam normalized relations with the United States in 1995, the same year it joined ASEAN.

display_title: Admittance to World Trade Organization
historical_status: Recent entries need external verification before publication
summary: Admittance to World Trade Organization.
